summary: Stancil riffs on watching Lou Gehrig’s retirement speech and realizing he is the same age, which sends him into compiling a sprawling, unnecessary spreadsheet of famous people’s ages at notable moments. The post uses that absurd catalog to poke at age-based comparison, mortality anxiety, and the futility of measuring life against milestones.
money_quotes: Until then, do your own calculus, and join me in making 101 comparisons that never needed to be made.
Are you here to read blog posts about data tooling and analytical best practices—like these bangers—not be reminded of your own looming mortality?
Ok, fair enough—though there is data in this post, I’m guessing that the data that most people are after isn’t the ages of the actors in Home Alone.
key_insight: Age comparisons across famous milestones are both irresistible and ultimately meaningless, revealing more about personal anxiety than about achievement.
